audioscript.text: Section 1, you will hear a man interviewing a woman in the street about her use of transport.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Excuse me.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Yes?
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: I wonder if you could spare a few minutes to do a survey on transport.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: It won't take long.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Uh. No, that's fine.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Oh, lovely.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: The survey is on behalf of the local council.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: They'd like to know about what transport you use, and any suggestions for improvement.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Uh. Can I start by asking you how you traveled to town today?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Sure, I came on the bus.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: The woman traveled to town by bus, so bus has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Excuse me.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Yes?
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: I wonder if you could spare a few minutes to do a survey on transport.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: It won't take long.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Uh. No, that's fine.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Oh, lovely.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: The survey is on behalf of the local council.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: They'd like to know about what transport you use, and any suggestions for improvement.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Uh. Can I start by asking you how you traveled to town today?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Sure, I came on the bus.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Great.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Now can I get a few details about yourself?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: OK, what's your name?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: It's Luisa.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Hardie.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Can you spell that please?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Yes, it's HARDIE.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Great, thanks, and can I have your address?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: It's 19 White Stone Road.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Oh, right, I know that area. It's Bradfield, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Is the postcode GT7?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: It's actually GT82LC.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Great, and could I ask what your job is?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Are you a student?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: I've actually just finished my training.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: I'm a hairdresser.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: All right, and one more question in this section.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: What is the reason for you coming into town today?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Actually, it's not for shopping today, which would be my normal reason.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Uh. But to see the dentist.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Right, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the interview, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, in this last section, I'd like you to give us some ideas about the facilities and arrangements in the city for getting to and from work.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Um. Any suggestions you have for improvements?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Well, something I've thought about for some time is that when I do walk, and I'm doing a later shift, I think the lighting should be better.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Yes, good point.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: And of course, I think it's a real shame they've been cutting down on the number of footpaths.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: They should have more of those.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Hmm. Then people would walk more.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Yes, right.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: And I don't think there are enough trains.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: That's why I don't use them.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: You have to wait so long.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Thanks, and finally, I'd like to ask your opinion on cycling.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: As you may know, there's a drive in the city to get more people to cycle to work.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: But we realize that there are things which the council, but also employers might do to help encourage workers to cycle to work.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Yep.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Well, I have thought about it, and where I work, there are no safe places to leave your bikes.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: OK, and also I'd have to cycle uphill, and on a hot day, I'd arrive at work pretty sweaty.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: So, I think I need a shower somewhere at work.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: And I suppose the last thing is that I wouldn't be all that confident about cycling on such busy roads.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: I think I'd like to see you offering training for that, you know, I'd feel a lot better about starting if that was the case.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Well, that's very helpful.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Uh. Thank you very much for your time.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: No problem.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: LUISA

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Transport Survey

audioscript.text: Section 2, you will hear a representative of a city council giving information about two new facilities which are opening soon in the city.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'm very pleased to have this opportunity to say a little about two exciting new developments in the city.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Brackenside Open-air Swimming Pool, and the children's adventure playground in Central Park.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As many of you may know, the idea for these initiatives came from you, the public, in the extensive consultation exercise which the City Council conducted last year.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And they have been realized using money from the SWRDC - the South West Regional Development Commission.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: First of all, Brackenside Pool.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As many of the older members of the audience will remember, there used to be a wonderful open air pool on the seafront 30 years ago, but it had to close when it was judged to be unsafe.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For the design of this new heated pool, we were very happy to secure the talents of internationally renowned architect, Ellen Wendon, who has managed to combine a charming 1930s design which fits so well with many of the other buildings in the area.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: With up to the minute features such as a recycling system, the only one of its kind in the world, which enables seawater to be used in the pool.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's been quite a bit of discussion in the local press about whether there would be enough room for the number of visitors we're hoping to attract, but the design is deceptive, and there have been rigorous checks about capacity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. Also just in case you were wondering, we're on schedule for June 15th opening date, and well within budget, a testimony to the excellent work of local contractors Hickman's.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We hope that as many people as possible will be there on June 15th.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We have engaged award winning actress Coral White to declare the pool open, and there'll be drinks and snacks available at the poolside.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There'll also be a competition for the public to decide on the sculpture we plan to have at the entrance.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You will decide which famous historical figure from the city we should have.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: And now, moving on to the Central Park playground, which we're pleased to announce has just won the Douglas Award for safety.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The news came through only last week.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The unique design is based on the concept of the Global Village, with the playground being divided into six areas showing different parts of the world, each with a representative feature.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, there is a section on Asia, and this is represented by rides and equipment in the shape of snakes, orangutans, tigers, and so on - fauna native to the forests of the region.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Moving south to the Antarctic, we couldn't run to an ice rink, I'm afraid, but opted instead for climbing blocks in the shape of mountains.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I thought they could have had slides for the glaciers, but the designers did want to avoid being too literal.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then onto South America, and here the theme is El Dorado, games replicating the search for mines full of precious stones, and then moving up to North America.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Here there was considerable debate.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I know the contribution of cinema and jazz was considered, but the designers finally opted for rockets and the International Space Station.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Eastwards to Europe then, and perhaps the most traditional choice of all the areas, medieval castles and other fortifications, then last but not least, moving south to Africa.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And a whole set of wonderful mosaics and trails to represent the great rivers of this fascinating and varied continent.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, the opening date for our Global Playground is the 10th of July. And again, we'd love to see you there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, make a date and come and see this magnificent original new amenity right in the heart of the city.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

text: Which feature is related to each of the following areas of the world represented in the playground?

audioscript.text: Section 3, you will hear two anthropology students called Victor and Olivia discussing their joint presentation about a Norwegian explorer called Thor Hyerdahl.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Right, well, for our presentation, shall I start with the early life of Thor Heyerdahl?
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: Why don't you begin with describing the type of boy he was?
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: Especially his passion for collecting things.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: That's right, he had his own little museum.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: And I think it's unusual for children to develop their own values, and not join in their parents' hobbies.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: I'm thinking of how Heyerdahl wouldn't go hunting with his dad for example.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: Yeah, he preferred to learn about nature by listening to his mother read to him, and quite early on, he knew he wanted to become an explorer when he grew up.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: That came from his camping trips he went on in Norway, I think.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: No, it was climbing that he spent his time on as a young man.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: Oh, right.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: After university, he married a classmate, and together, they decided to experience living on a small island to find out how harsh weather conditions shaped people's lifestyles.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: As part of their preparation, before they left home, they learned basic survival skills like building a shelter.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: I guess they needed that knowledge in order to live wild in a remote location.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: With few inhabitants cut off by the sea, which is what they were aiming to do.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: An important part of your talk should be the radical theory Heyerdahl formed from examining mysterious ancient carvings that he happened to find on the island.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: I think you should finish with that.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: All right, Victor.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: So after your part, I'll talk about Thor Heyerdahl's adult life, continuing from the theory he had about Polynesian migration.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: Up until that time, of course, academics had believed that humans first migrated to the islands in Polynesia from Asia in the west.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: Yes, they thought that travel from the east was impossible.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: Because of the huge empty stretch of ocean that lies between the islands and the nearest inhabited land.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, but Heyerdahl spent ages studying the cloud movements, ocean currents, and wind patterns to find if it was actually possible.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: And another argument was that there was no tradition of large ship building in the communities lying to the east of Polynesia, but Heyerdahl knew they made lots of coastal voyages in locally built canoes.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: Yes, or sailing on rafts, as was shown by the long voyage that Heyerdahl did next.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: It was an incredibly risky journey to undertake.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: Sometimes I wonder if he did that trip for private reasons, you know.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: To show others that he could have spectacular adventures.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: What do you think, Olivia?
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: Well, I think it was more a matter of simply trying out his idea, to see if migration from the east was possible.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: Yes, that's probably it.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: And the poor guy suffered a bit at that time, because the war forced him to stop his work for some years.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, uh, when he got started again and planned his epic voyage, do you think it was important to him that he achieve it before anyone else did?
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: Um. I haven't read anywhere that that was his motivation.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: The most important factor seems to have been that he use only ancient techniques and local materials to build his raft.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, I wonder how fast it went.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: Well, it took them 97 days from South America to the Pacific Islands.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: And after that, Heyerdahl went to Easter Island, didn't he?
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: We should mention the purpose of that trip.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: I think he sailed there in a boat made out of reeds.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: No, that was later on in Egypt, Olivia.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: But what he wanted to do was talk to the local people about their old stone carvings, and then make one himself.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: To learn more about the process.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: I see.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: Well what a great life, even though many of his theories have been disproven, he certainly left a lasting impression on many disciplines, didn't he?
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: To my mind, he was the first person to establish what modern academics call practical archaeology.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: I mean that they try to recreate something from the past today.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: Like he did with his raft trip.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: It's unfortunate that his ideas about where Polynesians originated from have been completely discredited.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: Right, well, I'll prepare a PowerPoint slide at the end that acknowledges our sources.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: I mainly used The Life and Work of Thor Heyerdahl by William Oliver.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: I thought the research methods he used were very sound, although I must say I found the overall tone somewhat old-fashioned.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: I think they need to do a new revised edition.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: Yeah, I agree.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: What about the subject matter?
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: I found it really challenging.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: Well, it's a complex issue.
audioscript.speaker: VICTOR

audioscript.text: I thought the book had lots of good points.
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: What did you think of...
audioscript.speaker: OLIVIA

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO hobbies was Thor Heyerdahl very interested in as a youth?
Which do the speakers say are the TWO reasons why Heyerdahl went to live on an island?
options: to study the impact of an extreme environment

items.title: According to Victor and Olivia, academics thought that Polynesian migration from the east was impossible due to
options: the fact that Eastern countries were far away.
the lack of materials for boat building.
the direction of the winds and currents.

items.title: Which do the speakers agree was the main reason for Heyerdahl's raft journey?
What was most important to Heyerdahl about his raft journey?
options: the fact that he was the first person to do it
the speed of crossing the Pacific
the use of authentic construction methods

items.title: Why did Heyerdahl go to Easter Island?
In Olivia's opinion, Heyerdahl's greatest influence was on
options: establishing archaeology as an academic subject.

items.title: Which criticism do the speakers make of William Oliver's textbook?
options: Its style is out of date.

body.title: The later life of Thor Heyerdahl

audioscript.text: Section 4, you will hear a business student predicting how management might change in the next few years.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Well, I've been talking to managers in a number of businesses, and reading surveys about the future of management.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And what I'm going to present in this seminar is a few ideas about how the activity is likely to change in the next 10 years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It isn't a scientific statistical analysis, just some ideas for us to discuss.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One area I want to mention is business markets, and I'm sure a really significant development will be a major increase in competition with companies from all around the world trying to sell similar products.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Consumers will have much more choice, for instance, food products sold in Australia might be manufactured in the USA, China, Finland, and dozens of other countries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At the same time, mergers and takeovers mean that governments are actually losing power to major global corporations.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We can probably all think of companies that exert a great deal of influence, which may be good for consumers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A third point I want to make about markets is that in the rapidly expanding economies such as India, China, Brazil and Russia, demand is growing very fast.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is putting pressure on resources all over the world.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I think businesses are becoming more open to external influences, in particular, companies are consulting customers more and more before making their business decisions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Companies are finding out what they want and providing it, instead of making products and then trying to sell them, which is the model of years ago.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another influence is that concerns about the environment will force manufacturers to extend product life cycles to reduce the amount of pollution and waste.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And in some cases, regulation will need to be strengthened.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many societies are much more fluid and democratic, and the structure of companies is changing to reflect that.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I think we're going to see a greater emphasis within companies on teams created with a specific project in mind.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And when they're completed, the teams will be disbanded and new ones formed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: More and more people see work as simply one part of their lifestyle.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And not the most important one, and as the workforce is shrinking in some countries, businesses are having to compete for staff instead of being able to choose among a lot of applicants.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Typical examples that will attract and retain staff are traditional ones like flexible hours, and something that has been made possible by advances in technology.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Remote working, with people based at their home, abroad, or almost anywhere they choose.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Management styles will almost certainly continue to change.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Senior managers will require a lot more than the efficiency that they've always needed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Above all, they'll need great skills in leadership, so that their organization can initiate and respond to change in a fast-moving world, where they face lots of competing requirements and potential conflicts.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In most of the world, the senior managers of large businesses are mainly men in their 50s and 60s.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The predominant style of management will almost certainly become more consultative and collaborative.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Caused, above all, by more women moving into senior management positions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many of the changes are influenced by developments in the wider economy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The traditional emphasis of business was manufacturing, and of course the service sector is very important.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But we shouldn't overlook the growing financial contribution of IP.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is intellectual property.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some books and films generate enormous sums from the sale of related DVDs, music, games, clothes, and so on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another point I'd like to make is that although I've been talking about companies, one trend that they have to face is the move away from people working for the same employer for years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Instead, more and more people are becoming self-employed to gain the freedom and control over their lives that they're unlikely to get from being employed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: OK, well, that's all I wanna say, so let's open it up for discussion.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER
